title: Setting Safe Current Limits
learningObjectives: Use resistance to cap current for sensitive loads
Relate Ohm's Law to milliamp targets
See how doubling resistance halves the current
introText: Every real circuit has a safe current limit. You'll start with a strong current, then use higher resistance to bring it down to a target range.

steps.instructionText: Place a 9V source and a 150Ω resistor in series. Wire the loop and check the current.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is above 50 mA — that's more than many LEDs can handle.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Lower the resistor value toward 150Ω or raise the voltage until current exceeds 50 mA.

steps.instructionText: Increase resistance to about 360Ω to reduce current below 25 mA.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Great — current is under 25 mA, a safe range for indicator LEDs.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise the resistor value. More resistance in series drops the current.

steps.instructionText: Keep the loop as-is and confirm the total power stays under 0.3 W.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Power is within limits. Lower current also protects against overheating.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: If power is high, increase resistance or lower the source voltage slightly.

questions.questionText: If you double the resistance in a series circuit while keeping voltage the same, what happens to current?
questions.explanation: Ohm's Law: I = V/R. Doubling R halves I.

questions.questionText: Why is setting a current target important for LEDs?
options: LEDs need as much current as possible
Too much current overheats and damages LEDs
Current has no effect on LEDs
questions.explanation: LEDs have safe current limits. Exceeding them can overheat or destroy the device.
